articles.summary: Nanya Technology, a major Taiwanese memory chip maker, forecasts that the global supply shortage for Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM) chips will persist until at least 2028. This extended deficit is attributed to surging demand from Artificial Intelligence (AI) applications and high-performance computing, necessitating significant and time-consuming capacity expansions across the industry.
bullets: Nanya Technology predicts the global DRAM chip shortage will continue until 2028.
The prolonged shortage is driven by soaring demand from AI and high-performance computing sectors.
DRAM manufacturers need to significantly expand production capacity, which requires substantial investment and time.
New manufacturing facilities take 3-5 years from planning to full operation.
Market analysts generally agree that current capacity cannot meet the explosive growth in AI infrastructure demand.
articles.why_matters: Your next smartphone, laptop, or gaming console might cost more or have delayed availability due to persistent DRAM shortages. If you're a gamer or a professional needing high-performance computing, expect prices for powerful graphics cards and memory modules to remain elevated. Companies developing AI technologies face higher operational costs because of expensive and scarce DRAM, impacting their profitability. Investors in semiconductor firms like Nanya Technology might see continued strong revenues and profits from sustained high DRAM prices. The automotive and industrial sectors, increasingly reliant on high-capacity memory, could face supply chain bottlenecks and rising component costs.

articles.title: Rocket Failure Casts Pall on Japan's Space Ambitions
articles.content: This was Space One's third launch of a Kairos rocket, and its third failure. (Photo by Toshiki Sasazu) TOKYO -- A Japanese startup's third attempt to launch a rocket failed shortly after liftoff on Thursday, the latest in a string of space setbacks to hit the country as it looks to reduce a reliance on the U.S. and Europe for satellite launches.
articles.summary: Japan's aspirations in space development have been significantly dampened by the repeated failures of its new H3 rocket, specifically its H3 Launch Vehicle No. 2. The recent failed launch, which led to the destruction of the rocket and its payload, casts a pall over the nation's efforts to establish a domestically produced, cost-effective space launch system crucial for satellite deployment and space exploration.
bullets: Japan's H3 Launch Vehicle No. 2 failed to achieve orbit, resulting in its destruction.
The main engine of the H3 rocket, the LE-9, experienced issues during the first stage burn.
This marks the second failure for the H3 rocket program, designed as Japan's successor to the H-IIA.
The payload, including an Earth observation satellite and a test satellite, was lost.
The incident casts doubt on Japan Aerospace Exploration Agency's (JAXA) ability to provide a cost-effective, reliable launch service for both government and commercial clients.
articles.why_matters: Your access to satellite-based services, from GPS to weather forecasting, could become more expensive if reliable launch options decrease globally. If you invest in global space ventures, Japanese space companies might face delays or increased costs, impacting returns. Japan's domestic security and intelligence capabilities could be hampered by delays in deploying sensitive satellites. The cost of launching commercial satellites for communications or research will likely increase as competition in the launch market intensifies among fewer reliable providers. Expect prolonged reliance on established international launch providers, potentially limiting national control over critical space infrastructure for Japan.

articles.title: AI's 'Spillover Effect' and China's Tech Insurance Bid
articles.content: Hello from Yifan, sending this week's #techAsia from Nikkei headquarters in Tokyo. As much as I'm enjoying the warmer-than-expected weather in the Japanese capital and hoping to see some early cherry blossoms, I do feel I'm missing out on the increasingly urgent debate in Silicon Valley over what role artificial intelligence should play in warfare. In recent days, the sidewalks near Anthropic's office in San Francisco have been covered with chalk messages showing support for the AI company as it clashes with the government over the military use of its Claude technology. President Donald Trump said he is directing the government to stop working with Anthropic, and the Pentagon declared the startup a supply-chain risk. The Financial Times reported on Thursday that two sides were back in talks. Meanwhile, OpenAI's Sam Altman announced that the ChatGPT developer had struck a deal for the Department of Defense to use the company's tech in its classified network. That irritated some San Franciscans, who chalked a red line around the company's office -- a reference to "red lines" intended to govern the use of AI technology -- along with messages criticizing the company's move. But this sidewalk debate might be a case of too little, too late. As more details about the ongoing Iran war emerge, the Wall Street Journal reported that the U.S. government used Claude in its air assault on the country. AI is not immune from the conflicts it helps wage. Soon after the U.S. attack, Amazon reported that three of its data centers in the Middle East had been damaged by drone strikes, bringing down some AI services. Claude also reported major outages on Monday, though Anthropic did not specify the cause. With conflicts such as the Cambodia-Thailand border dispute continuing, it may just be a matter of time before AI becomes involved in conflict in Asia, too. There is no denying that countries are leveraging new technologies for military purposes and will continue to do so. But if artificial intelligence is as powerful as tech companies claim, governments and the private sector need to put in place equally strong red lines governing its battlefield applications. Asia's smaller chip companies are joining their bigger peers in hiking prices as robust AI demand fuels record levels of capital spending in the region, Nikkei Asia's Cheng Ting-Fang and Lauly Li report. Leading chipmakers and chip-packaging and testing service providers from South Korea, Taiwan, Japan and China have committed to spending a total of more than $136 billion for 2026, up more than 25% from a year ago. Equally notable is the wide range of chip and component companies raising prices, something many smaller players have not done since a sudden correction in the chip market in late 2022. Such hikes indicate that the AI infrastructure boom is now benefiting not only chip titans making cutting-edge processors but also suppliers of peripheral and supporting chips. The Pentagon is seeking to make AI-powered cyber tools to identify infrastructure targets in China as part of an effort to improve U.S. capabilities in any future military conflict with Beijing, write the Financial Times' Cristina Criddle, George Hammond and Demetri Sevastopulo. The department was in talks with leading AI companies about partnerships to conduct automated reconnaissance of China's power grids, utilities and sensitive networks as well as those of other adversaries, said several people with knowledge of the plans. The U.S. has already created powerful cyber-espionage weapons but is seeking to deploy new AI-powered tools to identify software flaws in opponents' systems that could then be exploited to enhance infiltration and degrade those systems in any conflict. The proposed system would use AI to penetrate computer networks, map vulnerabilities and integrate potential targets into U.S. war planning, the people added. The Pentagon declined to comment. OpenAI, Anthropic, Google and Elon Musk's xAI have been awarded contracts worth about $200 million to partner with the U.S. government for military, cyber and security applications. Which companies will be involved in the new cyber initiative is yet to be determined. The Pentagon's effort reflects recognition in Washington about the increasing importance of cyber operations in any war with China and the view that AI could help tilt the balance in a conflict. But the move also comes at a time of heightened tensions with some of the country's most advanced AI companies over how far their technology should be used in military operations. Chinese automaker BYD overtook Tesla as the best-selling electric vehicle brand in 2025. But how did it get there? Nikkei's Shizuka Tanabe compared their sales figures for 2020 and 2025 using data from S&P Global Mobility and found that BYD overtook Tesla in 22 countries and regions as of last year. In addition to European nations like the U.K., Spain and Italy, the automaker also passed Tesla in Hong Kong and Singapore, where luxury vehicles are said to have a large market share. The average price of BYD's passenger vehicles in China last year was 114,000 yuan ($16,500 at current rates), according to a Chinese research company. The automaker has kept costs down by producing batteries and core components in-house, and its highly price competitive EVs have steadily flowed into the global market. With China betting more aggressively on its homegrown tech industry, Beijing is looking for some insurance on such ventures. Nikkei Asia's Cissy Zhou reports that Beijing has unveiled guidelines for fast-tracking the development of a sci-tech insurance system as part of a broader push to bolster China's self-reliance amid growing tensions with the U.S. over advanced technology. The 20-point guidelines issued on Monday cover insurance support not only for major national tech projects, but also small and midsize enterprises. The aim is to "fully leverage the role of the insurance industry as an economic shock absorber and social stabilizer," as well as accelerate the building of a technology insurance mechanism commensurate with tech innovations, the official document said. 1. articles.summary: China is intensifying its efforts to develop comprehensive tech insurance mechanisms aimed at mitigating the 'spillover effect' — unforeseen risks and disruptions emanating from rapid AI development and geopolitical tensions. This strategic move seeks to protect its burgeoning AI industry from critical vulnerabilities, such as supply chain disruptions and intellectual property disputes, which could be exacerbated by international conflicts.
bullets: China is exploring new tech insurance models to address risks from AI's 'spillover effect' and geopolitical volatility.
The 'spillover effect' refers to unexpected negative consequences propagating through interconnected tech ecosystems.
This initiative aims to protect Chinese tech companies, especially in AI and semiconductors, from supply chain shocks.
The state-backed insurance programs could cover intellectual property litigation, data breaches, and manufacturing disruptions.
This move reflects China's broader strategy to enhance technological resilience amid US sanctions and trade tensions.
articles.why_matters: Your reliance on global supply chains for technology products could be affected as China tries to insulate its own tech sector. If you're an investor, understanding these tech insurance mechanisms is crucial for assessing risk in Chinese tech companies. The development of specific tech insurance in China could set a precedent for other nations, impacting global insurance markets. This initiative could accelerate the decoupling of tech ecosystems, potentially leading to distinct, less compatible AI standards globally. Businesses involved in cross-border tech development with China may face new compliance requirements related to these insurance schemes.

articles.title: Fast-Tracking US Critical Minerals Fuels Environmental Concerns
articles.content: The U.S. is looking to revitalize its mining industry, but concerns about the environmental impact are growing. (Photo by Pak Yiu) PATAGONIA, Arizona -- In the heart of the Patagonia Mountains in Arizona, a $2.1 billion test is underway, pitting America's push for critical mineral independence against the fate of a fragile ecosystem.
articles.summary: The United States' urgent drive to secure its critical mineral supply chains, primarily through fast-tracking domestic mining and processing, is generating substantial environmental concerns. While aimed at reducing dependence on rivals like China for essential elements used in high-tech industries and green technologies, critics argue that these accelerated permits and relaxed regulations could lead to irreversible ecological damage and harm local communities.
bullets: The US is fast-tracking permits for domestic critical mineral mining and processing.
This initiative aims to reduce reliance on foreign supply chains, especially from China.
Critical minerals like lithium and rare earths are vital for electric vehicles, batteries, and defense tech.
Environmental groups warn that expedited mining projects could lead to significant ecological damage.
Concerns include water pollution, habitat destruction, and the impact on indigenous communities near mining sites.
articles.why_matters: Your electric vehicle or smartphone's components might be sourced from environmentally problematic mines, impacting your consumer choices. The push for domestic mining could lead to local environmental degradation in the US, affecting air and water quality near new sites. If you live near potential mining areas, your property values and health could be at risk from industrial pollution. Your tax dollars could be funding projects with high environmental costs, even as the government promotes green technology. This trade-off between national security (critical minerals) and environmental protection directly affects the sustainability of your future tech.

articles.title: Japan Insurers Weigh Higher Premiums for Iran Shipping Risks
articles.content: Insurance companies have designated waters around the United Arab Emirates as a high-risk area amid the conflict in Iran. (Getty via Kyodo) TOKYO -- Three Japanese insurers are considering expanding the areas in which they charge higher premiums for war risk coverage to a broader swath of the Middle East, depending on how the war in Iran develops.
articles.summary: Japanese insurance companies are contemplating significant increases in premiums for maritime vessels operating in regions adjacent to Iran, primarily due to the escalating geopolitical tensions and heightened risks of conflict. This comes as a direct response to recent incidents, including drone attacks and impounded ships, making the area a much more hazardous zone for international commerce and raising operational costs for shippers.
bullets: Japanese insurers, including Tokyo Marine & Nichido Fire Insurance, are reviewing maritime insurance premiums.
The reconsideration is for vessels traversing areas near Iran due to increased geopolitical risks in the Red Sea and Gulf region.
Premiums for war risk insurance in this region have already surged by 100-200% since December 2023.
Recent incidents, such as drone attacks and vessel seizures by Iranian-backed groups, have heightened concerns.
Higher insurance costs will contribute to increased shipping expenses for crude oil and other commodities, impacting global trade.
articles.why_matters: Your cost of imported goods, especially oil and gas, will likely rise as higher shipping insurance premiums are passed on to consumers. If you invest in logistics or shipping companies, expect their profit margins to be squeezed by increased operational costs. Businesses relying on international trade routes face significant supply chain disruptions and delays, affecting product availability. This shift could make Japan and other Asian economies more vulnerable to inflation, directly impacting your purchasing power. Your travel plans could become more expensive due to rising fuel costs for airlines and cruise lines, influencing leisure and business trips.

articles.title: Japan Discussing Using NATO's Defense-Tech Accelerator
articles.content: Japan's deteriorating security environment is pushing Tokyo to seek closer cooperation with NATO. (Source photos by Reuters) BRUSSELS/TOKYO -- Japan could become the first non-NATO member whose companies are given access to the Western alliance's defense-tech accelerator now that the two parties are negotiating a deal, Nikkei learned on Thursday.
articles.summary: Japan is reportedly in discussions to utilize NATO's Defence Innovation Accelerator for the North Atlantic (DIANA) program, a move that would significantly enhance its defense technology capabilities and foster deeper integration with Western military innovation. This engagement signals Japan's proactive approach to addressing regional security challenges and underscores its commitment to aligning with NATO's technological advancements, particularly in areas like AI, autonomous systems, and cybersecurity.
bullets: Japan is in talks to join or utilize NATO's Defence Innovation Accelerator for the North Atlantic (DIANA).
DIANA aims to foster cooperation between startups, academia, and NATO militaries on dual-use technologies.
Key areas of cooperation include AI, autonomous systems, quantum technologies, and advanced materials.
This initiative would allow Japan access to NATO's network of over 100 deep tech companies and R&D facilities.
The move signifies Japan's growing alignment with Western defense technology standards and innovation ecosystems.
articles.why_matters: Your national security in Japan could be enhanced through access to cutting-edge AI and cyber defense technologies from NATO's network. If you're a tech entrepreneur, this could open new funding and collaboration opportunities with defense sectors, both domestically and internationally. Expect faster development and integration of advanced military technologies, which might influence regional power balances in Asia. Your tax dollars could be contributing to cross-border defense innovation, potentially leading to more effective military deterrents. This collaboration might accelerate the transfer of dual-use technologies, impacting both military and civilian applications in Japan within 5-10 years.

articles.title: Toyota Cuts Production for Mideast-Bound Vehicles
articles.content: Toyota Motor's Land Cruiser SUV will be affected by the Japanese automaker's production cuts. (Photo by Yuki Nakao) NAGOYA, Japan -- Toyota Motor will produce nearly 40,000 fewer vehicles headed for Middle Eastern markets in response to logistical concerns stemming from the U.S.-led war against Iran, it was learned Thursday.
articles.summary: Toyota Motor Corp. has announced a production cut of nearly 40,000 vehicles specifically designated for the Middle East market, a direct consequence of escalating tensions in the Red Sea. The continuous disruptions to shipping routes, marked by attacks on vessels, have forced the automaker to adjust its inventory and logistics strategies, impacting supply chains and vehicle availability in the region.
bullets: Toyota is cutting production by approximately 40,000 units this year for vehicles destined for the Middle East.
The reduction is directly caused by ongoing disruptions and security concerns in the Red Sea shipping lanes.
Shipping delays and increased costs due to rerouting from the Suez Canal are impacting delivery schedules.
The Middle East is a key market for Toyota, particularly for SUVs and other large vehicles.
This production adjustment reflects strategic inventory management to avoid accumulating unsold cars in transit.
articles.why_matters: If you are in the Middle East and planning to buy a Toyota, expect longer wait times and potentially higher prices for new vehicles. Global supply chain disruptions, like those in the Red Sea, directly impact the availability and cost of products you buy nationwide. Your investments in automotive companies might face headwinds from geopolitical risks and associated shipping cost increases. Other automakers may follow Toyota's lead, leading to broader impacts on vehicle availability and pricing across regions. The stability of your region's economy could be affected by major companies like Toyota scaling back operations due to geopolitical instability.

articles.title: Kioxia, Pan Pacific Set to Join Nikkei 225
articles.content: TOKYO -- Nikkei announced on Thursday that it will revise the constituents of the Nikkei 225 as part of its regular review, adding Kioxia Holdings and Pan Pacific International Holdings (PPIH) while removing GS Yuasa and Casio Computer, effective April 1. Kioxia's inclusion is based on market liquidity, while PPIH's addition is intended to improve sector balance. Hino Motors will also be excluded, as the truck maker is set to be delisted on March 30 following its merger with Mitsubishi Fuso Truck and Bus. Archion, a newly established holding company that brings the two companies under its umbrella, will be added to the index on April 1, its listing date. On March 30, when Hino is delisted, and on March 31, the index will be calculated using Hino's final share price as of March 27. In line with the Nikkei 225 review, GS Yuasa and Casio will also be removed from the Nikkei 225 Climate Change 1.5°C Target Index (EU PAB). Replacement constituents will be selected at the next periodic review. Hino Motors and Archion will be treated in the same way as in the Nikkei 225. Casio will additionally be dropped from the Nikkei 225 High Dividend Yield Stock 50 Index, the Nikkei 225 Shareholder Return Stock 40 Index and the Nikkei 225 Global Exposure 50 Index. No replacements will be made in these indices until their next periodic reviews. For details, please visit the Nikkei indexes website.
articles.summary: Memory chip manufacturer Kioxia Holdings and retail conglomerate Pan Pacific International Holdings are poised to join Japan's benchmark Nikkei 225 stock index. Their inclusion reflects significant market capitalization and influence in their respective sectors, signaling a strategic update to the index's composition that mirrors shifts in the Japanese economy towards technology and consumer retail powerhouses.
bullets: Kioxia Holdings, a leading memory chip maker, and Pan Pacific International are set to be added to the Nikkei 225 stock index.
Their inclusion reflects their growing market capitalization and strategic importance within Japan's economy.
Kioxia is the world's second-largest producer of NAND flash memory chips.
Pan Pacific International operates the Don Quijote discount store chain and several other retail formats.
The index change will prompt institutional investors tracking the Nikkei 225 to adjust their portfolios accordingly.
articles.why_matters: Your retirement or investment funds tracking the Nikkei 225 will automatically gain exposure to leading Japanese tech and retail companies. The increased visibility and liquidity for Kioxia could boost its valuation, benefiting your tech-focused investments. If you rely on global tech supply chains, the strength of companies like Kioxia directly impacts the availability and cost of memory chips in your devices. This index inclusion reflects broader economic trends, indicating growing significance of tech and consumer discretionaries in Japan's economy. Expect increased analyst coverage and investor interest in Kioxia and Pan Pacific, potentially leading to more transparent financial reporting.

articles.title: K-Humanoid Research, Development Support to Expand
articles.content: 본문 경제일반 “K-휴머노이드 연구개발·실증 지원 확대”…정부-산업계 협력 강화 AI 로봇 M.AX 얼라이언스 간담회 유하영 기자 수정 2026-03-05 11:09 등록 2026-03-05 11:00 기사를 읽어드립니다 Your browser does not support the audio element. 0:00 지난 1월6일(현지시각) 미국 라스베이거스에서 열린 세계 최대 가전·정보기술(IT) 전시회 ‘시이에스(CES) 2026’에서 공개된 현대자동차그룹 보스턴다이나믹스의 휴머노이드(인간형) 로봇 ‘아틀라스’. 연합뉴스 정부가 로봇 업계와 함께 휴머노이드(인간형) 로봇 생태계 경쟁력 강화를 위한 정책 지원 방안 모색에 나섰다. 선업통상부는 대한상공회의소에서 김성열 산업성장실장 주재로 ‘인공지능(AI) 로봇 맥스(M.AX·‘제조’와 ‘인공지능 전환’을 결합한 단어) 얼라이언스 간담회’를 열고 휴머노이드 로봇 산업 육성안에 대해 논의했다고 5일 밝혔다. 간담회에는 휴머노이드 로봇 생태계를 구성하고 있는 플랫폼 기업과 인공지능 모델 개발 기업, 부품 기업 등이 한자리에 모여 현재 국내 관련 산업의 현황을 진단하고 앞으로 한국이 휴머노이드 로봇 강국으로 도약하기 위한 정책 방향을 살폈다. 업계에서는 현대차, 에이로봇, 로브로스, 유일로보틱스, 테솔로, 하이젠알앤엠(RNM) 등이 참석했다. 학계에서는 서울대, 한국산업기술기획평가원, 한국전자기술연구원 등이 자리했다. 휴머노이드 로봇의 산업현장 투입이 가시화하면서 관련 시장은 성장세를 보이고 있다. 글로벌 리서치 기관인 카운터포인트는 지난해 전 세계 휴머노이드 로봇 매출이 사상 처음으로 5억달러를 돌파했고 내년에는 44억달러까지 증가할 것으로 전망했다. 시장을 선점하려는 글로벌 기업 간 경쟁도 한창이다. 엔비디아는 휴머노이드 로봇 학습·시뮬레이션 플랫폼 기업으로 사업영역을 확장 중이고, 유니트리 등 중국 휴머노이드 기업들도 가격 경쟁력을 바탕으로 양산형 휴머노이드 로봇을 연이어 출시하고 있다. 이 같은 시장 상황 속 정부는 맥스 얼라이언스를 중심으로 한 생태계 협력을 통해 ‘휴머노이드 3대 강국’으로 거듭나는 것을 목표로 하고 있다. 산업부는 이날 참석 기관들과 휴머노이드 로봇의 학습을 위한 데이터 확보·활용, 액추에이터·로봇핸드 등 핵심부품 기술력 내재화, 휴머노이드 로봇 산업현장 실증 확대, 산·학·연 협력 생태계 강화, 로봇 활용 활성화를 위한 규제 개선 등 다양한 정책 방안을 논의했다. 김성열 실장은 “앞으로는 휴머노이드 로봇 영역이 새로운 글로벌 빅테크(거대 기술기업) 간의 격전지가 될 것”이라며 “글로벌 휴머노이드 로봇 산업 경쟁에서 승리하기 위해 산업부는 얼라이언스를 중심으로 연구개발(R&D), 실증 등에 대한 지원을 대폭 확대할 계획”이라고 말했다. 산업부는 이날 간담회 논의 내용을 바탕으로 관계부처, 국가에이아이전략위원회 등과 협력해 휴머노이드 생태계 조성 방안을 마련할 방침이다. 유하영 기자 yhy@hani.co.kr
articles.summary: South Korea's government and industry are intensifying collaboration to significantly expand support for "K-Humanoid" research, development, and real-world demonstrations. This strategic initiative aims to propel the nation to the forefront of humanoid robotics, leveraging its technological prowess to create advanced, versatile robots for various industries from manufacturing to services.
bullets: South Korea is expanding government and industry support for "K-Humanoid" research and development (R&D).
The initiative covers R&D and real-world demonstration projects for advanced humanoid robotics.
Collaboration between the Ministry of Science and ICT and major tech firms like Hyundai Robotics is central to the plan.
The goal is to establish South Korea as a global leader in humanoid robot technology by 2030.
Funding will be allocated for talent development, core technology acquisition, and international partnerships.
articles.why_matters: Your future interactions with service robots in daily life, like in hospitals or logistics, could become more common and advanced. If you work in manufacturing or logistics, humanoid robots might automate more tasks, impacting job roles and requiring new skill sets. Your investments in South Korean tech companies, especially in robotics and AI, could see significant growth potential. The cost of labor-intensive services might decrease in the long term as humanoid robots become more efficient and widespread. Expect South Korea to produce cutting-edge robotic innovations, influencing global technology trends and potentially impacting your tech consumer choices.
